title: Rules targeting risk reduction in critical infrastructure sectors
full_text:
AI FUTURE IN PARTNERSHIP WITH TECHNOLOGY1 Last month’s new rules governing how many of Australia’s critical infrastructure operators manage risk, may look both technical and limited on the surface, but they pull many more companies and their suppliers into the legislative net and mark a fundamental shift in the way the government thinks about risk to our most vital industries.
At first glance, the Enhanced Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (E-CIRMP) rules that came into force on June 5 are just another a three-page amendment to the 2018 Security of Critical Infrastructure Act, but they move the act from expecting critical infrastructure companies and their suppliers from being aware of risks to stipulating how they treat those risks.
New requirements include raising so-called “maturity levels” to require measures like phishing-resistant multi-factor authentication and faster patching for vulnerabilities, with mapping supply chain vulnerabilities, mandatory AusCheck background checks on key personnel and explicit Foreign Ownership, Control or Influence (FOCI) risk management.
“There have been two kinds of expansions: horizontal, meaning more industries and more things – and vertical expansion down into the supply chain,” says Daniel Moulis, a lawyer who advises on the impact of the new regulations.
“The E-CIRMP upgrade will affect more companies in critical infrastructure supply chains, including all of the IT consultancies and personnel agencies etc that will need to be brought in by the directly affected companies to comply with all the cyber security red tape that is now mandatory.” The new enhanced rules specifically apply to companies and their suppliers operating critical infrastructure in nine important sectors: energy market operators, electricity assets, gas assets, liquid fuel assets, water, broadcasting, domain name systems, freight services and freight infrastructure.
Although the new E-CIRMP rules contain specific obligations for specific industries, it is also a clear forewarning that even companies on the fringe of the critical infrastructure ecosystem need to build adaptability and scalability into their risk management assessment and reporting frameworks.
“Compliance is only going to get more challenging as threats to critical infrastructure evolve and expand,” said Chris Polkinghorne, Head of Security and Compliance at TechnologyOne, Australia’s leading domestic supplier of enterprise software.
“More and more, we’re finding our clients are looking for suppliers who will share the burden of both hardening their networks and being able to show that they are complying with increasingly rigorous legislation like E-CIRMP.” Mike Burgess, the head of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation (ASIO) outlined the danger in his annual Threat Assessment speech last month.
“Critical infrastructure in the energy and communications sectors, as well as infrastructure supporting the military, are top targets,” he said. “We discovered nation state hackers had compromised the network of an Australian critical infrastructure provider.
ASIO assessed the hackers were preparing for sabotage. They weren’t planting ‘digital dynamite’ as such; they were mapping out the network and maintaining access so they could cripple it at a time of their choosing,” he said.
Burgess said the threat is expanding. “Many of the things we’ve taken for granted are being tested: social cohesion, economic stability, supply chain certainty amongst them.” Supply chains are one of the focal points of the new rules, but it is unclear how far Australian critical infrastructure users should, or can, dig into their supply chains.
All the new rules say is that critical infrastructure providers must probe their supply chains “as far as reasonably practicable” but with big fines in the offing, it is not unreasonable to assume there will be different interpretations of “reasonably practicable”, and many critical infrastructure operators might find it easier to go with a domestic software supplier and inherit their compliance and security credentials.
The issue of cyber sovereignty is not directly mentioned in the new regulations, but it looms large in the background.
“The rules aren’t demanding data sovereignty yet, in the sense of localisation and capability, but it’s hard to believe that’s not too far down the track.
Governments already require that of their own data. Ringfencing something that isn’t in your own country is difficult, and the contract partner rules only go part of the way,” says Moulis.
With the US decision to ban all foreign usage of Anthropic’s Mythos 5 and Fable 5 AI systems, including in Australia, it is clear that even our allies could weaponise access to critical systems.
The challenge that the government has set for the infrastructure providers is not just to respond to evolving threats as they appear, but to build a risk management architecture that can counter threats before they become critical.
“Risk should translate into action before the risk emerges rather than after,” as Daniel Moulis says.
He is blunt about the long-term solution. “Spend money constantly. Embed in your supply chain only organisations that are also spending money constantly. I’m a regulatory expert, not a cyber expert, but even I can see we’ve got a constantly evolving problem every time I turn on my email in the morning.”
